title: System Rescue & Recovery
description: Every senior engineer faces an unbootable server, a corrupt filesystem, or a forgotten root password at some point. System rescue skills — the ability to recover a system using recovery media, chroot, and manual intervention — separate engineers who can recover from disaster from those who have to reinstall. At Enterprise, automated recovery handles most cases, but when the automation fails, manual rescue skills are essential.

sections.title: Boot Process: From Power-On to Login Prompt
sections.description: Understanding the boot sequence lets you identify where a boot failure occurs. BIOS/UEFI → Bootloader (GRUB) → Kernel → initramfs → root filesystem → systemd (PID 1) → login. Each stage has specific failure modes. At Enterprise, the boot sequence is optimized for speed (fast boot targets, parallel service startup) but the fundamentals are the same.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Boot stage 1: Firmware (BIOS/UEFI) ===
# BIOS: legacy, MBR partition, 16-bit mode
# UEFI: modern, GPT partition, 64-bit, Secure Boot
# Check current mode:
[ -d /sys/firmware/efi ] && echo "UEFI" || echo "BIOS"
ls /sys/firmware/efi/efivars/          # UEFI variables (if UEFI)

# === Boot stage 2: Bootloader (GRUB) ===
# GRUB config: /boot/grub/grub.cfg (DO NOT EDIT — generated by update-grub)
# User config: /etc/default/grub (edit this, then run update-grub)

cat /etc/default/grub
# GRUB_DEFAULT=0                       # Default menu entry
# GRUB_TIMEOUT=5                       # Seconds before auto-boot
# GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=""               # Kernel boot parameters
# GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash"  # Additional params

# Common boot parameters:
# single            — Boot to single-user mode (recovery)
# emergency         — Boot to emergency shell
# init=/bin/bash    — Override init (skip systemd)
# nomodeset         — Disable kernel mode-setting (display issues)
# selinux=0         — Disable SELinux (troubleshooting only)
# systemd.unit=rescue.target  — Boot to rescue target

# Update GRUB after changes:
sudo update-grub                       # Debian/Ubuntu
sudo grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg  # RHEL/Amazon Linux

# === Boot stage 3: Kernel + initramfs ===
# initramfs (initial RAM filesystem) contains:
# - Essential kernel modules (storage, filesystem, network)
# - /init script that mounts root filesystem
# - Busybox or recovery tools

# List initramfs contents:
lsinitramfs /boot/initrd.img-$(uname -r)  # Ubuntu
lsinitrd /boot/initramfs-$(uname -r).img    # RHEL

# Rebuild initramfs:
sudo update-initramfs -u                 # Ubuntu
sudo dracut -f                           # RHEL/Amazon Linux

# === Boot stage 4: Root filesystem + systemd ===
# /init mounts rootfs, then pivots to real root
# systemd reads default.target → starts services
# Check default target:
systemctl get-default

sections.title: GRUB: Bootloader Rescue & Repair
sections.description: GRUB failure = system won't boot. Common causes: misconfigured grub.cfg, missing kernel, damaged MBR/GPT, or wrong root filesystem UUID. At Enterprise, boot failures are handled by automated recovery (PXE boot, IPMI console, AWS Systems Manager) — but manual GRUB rescue is the fallback.
sections.codeBlock:
# === GRUB rescue shell ===
# If GRUB can't find its config, you get:
# grub rescue>

# Commands in grub rescue:
grub rescue> ls                        # List available drives
# (hd0) (hd0,msdos1) (hd0,msdos2)
grub rescue> ls (hd0,msdos1)/         # List files on partition
grub rescue> set prefix=(hd0,msdos1)/boot/grub
grub rescue> set root=(hd0,msdos1)
grub rescue> insmod normal
grub rescue> normal                    # Load normal GRUB menu

# === GRUB interactive editing ===
# At GRUB menu, press 'e' to edit boot entry
# Common fixes:
# 1. Change root=UUID=... to root=/dev/sda1 (if UUID changed)
# 2. Add 'single' or 'emergency' to boot into recovery
# 3. Add 'init=/bin/bash' to skip init entirely
# 4. Fix broken root= parameter

# === Reinstall GRUB from live CD ===
# Boot from Ubuntu live USB, mount root filesystem:
sudo mkdir /mnt/root
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/root
sudo mount --bind /dev /mnt/root/dev
sudo mount --bind /proc /mnt/root/proc
sudo mount --bind /sys /mnt/root/sys

# Chroot and reinstall GRUB:
sudo chroot /mnt/root
grub-install /dev/sda
update-grub
exit
sudo umount -R /mnt/root
# Reboot

# === Fix broken kernel/boot files ===
# If /boot is empty (accidental rm -rf /boot):
# Boot from live CD, chroot, install new kernel:
apt-get install --reinstall linux-image-$(uname -r)
update-grub

# === GRUB password protection ===
grub-mkpasswd-pbkdf2                   # Generate password hash
# Add to /etc/grub.d/40_custom:
# set superusers="admin"
# password_pbkdf2 admin grub.pbkdf2.sha512.10000...
# Then update-grub

sections.title: Chroot Rescue & Single-User Mode
sections.description: When a system won't boot normally, you can boot from recovery media, mount the root filesystem, and chroot into it to repair packages, fix configuration files, reset passwords, or rebuild the bootloader. Single-user mode boots with minimum services and a root shell. At Enterprise, these techniques are documented in runbooks and tested during disaster recovery drills.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Single-user mode ===
# At GRUB prompt, edit boot entry, add 'single' to kernel line
# Press Ctrl+X to boot
# You get a root shell with minimal services
# Remount root as read-write:
mount -o remount,rw /

# Fix the issue, then reboot

# === Emergency mode ===
# Even more minimal than single-user
# At GRUB: add 'emergency' or 'systemd.unit=emergency.target'
# Only PID 1 runs, root filesystem is read-only

# === Chroot rescue (from live USB) ===
# Step 1: Boot from Ubuntu/Debian live USB
# Step 2: Identify and mount root:
lsblk                                    # Find root partition
sudo mkdir /mnt/root
sudo mount /dev/sda2 /mnt/root           # Mount root filesystem

# Step 3: Mount essential filesystems:
sudo mount --bind /dev /mnt/root/dev
sudo mount --bind /proc /mnt/root/proc
sudo mount --bind /sys /mnt/root/sys
sudo mount --bind /run /mnt/root/run     # Some systems need this

# Step 4: Mount boot partition if separate:
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/root/boot

# Step 5: Chroot:
sudo chroot /mnt/root /bin/bash
source /etc/profile
export PS1="(chroot) \[\e[31m\]\u@\h:\w\$\[\e[0m\] "

# === Inside chroot: common repairs ===
# Reset root password:
passwd root

# Fix broken packages:
apt-get install -f

# Reinstall bootloader:
grub-install /dev/sda
update-grub

# Rebuild initramfs:
update-initramfs -u -k all

# Fix filesystem:
e2fsck -f /dev/sda2

# === Exit chroot ===
exit
sudo umount -R /mnt/root
sudo reboot

sections.title: Filesystem Recovery & Data Rescue
sections.description: Filesystem corruption can result from power loss, hardware failure, or improper shutdown. ext4 has a journal for fast recovery; XFS has metadata writeback logging. At Enterprise, filesystem corruption is rare (EBS volumes have 99.999% durability, proper shutdown procedures are enforced), but when it happens, the response is methodical: assess damage → backup → repair → restore.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Check filesystem health ===
# Check without modifying (safe):
sudo fsck -n /dev/sdb1                   # ext4: check only, no repair
sudo xfs_repair -n /dev/sdb1             # XFS: check only

# === ext4 journal recovery ===
# ext4 has a journal that records metadata changes
# On unclean shutdown: journal is replayed on mount (automatic)
# If journal is corrupt:
sudo fsck.ext4 -f /dev/sdb1              # Force check
sudo fsck.ext4 -fy /dev/sdb1             # Force + yes (non-interactive)

# === XFS repair ===
# XFS must be unmounted for repair
sudo xfs_repair /dev/sdb1
sudo xfs_repair -v /dev/sdb1             # Verbose
sudo xfs_repair -L /dev/sdb1             # Clear log (DANGER: may lose recent metadata)

# === Bad blocks ===
# Check for hardware-level bad sectors:
sudo badblocks -sv /dev/sdb1
# If bad blocks found: replace the drive!
# For EBS: snapshot, create new volume, detach old

# === SMART data (hardware health) ===
sudo smartctl -a /dev/sda                # Full SMART report
sudo smartctl -H /dev/sda                # Overall health (PASSED/FAILED)
# Key SMART attributes:
# 5: Reallocated_Sector_Ct (> 0 = bad!)
# 187: Reported_Uncorrectable_Errors
# 197: Current_Pending_Sector
# 198: Offline_Uncorrectable

# === ddrescue (recover data from failing disk) ===
sudo apt install gddrescue
sudo ddrescue -d /dev/sdb /dev/sdc rescue.log
# Retry failed sectors:
sudo ddrescue -d -r3 /dev/sdb /dev/sdc rescue.log

# === Backup superblock (ext4) ===
sudo mkfs.ext4 -n /dev/sdb1              # Show backup superblock locations
sudo fsck.ext4 -b 32768 /dev/sdb1       # Use alternate superblock

# === XFS backup superblock ===
sudo xfs_db -x -c "sb 0" -c "p" /dev/sdb1  # Check primary superblock
sudo xfs_repair -b 1 /dev/sdb1          # Use backup superblock (1, 2, 4, 8, ...)

# === If data is critical ===
# 1. STOP: don't mount the filesystem, don't run fsck
# 2. Create a block-level backup:
sudo dd if=/dev/sdb1 of=/backup/sdb1.img bs=1M status=progress
# 3. Work on the backup, not the original
# 4. Use testdisk or photorec for file recovery
# 5. At Enterprise: snapshot EBS volume, then repair snapshot

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'A server won't boot. You can't SSH in. What do you do?'

A: 'First, access the console out-of-band — IPMI/iLO/iDRAC for bare metal, AWS Console → EC2 Serial Console for cloud, or recovery mode for VMs. Check what stage it reaches: (1) Power-on: does it POST? Check power supply, IPMI status. (2) Bootloader: can you see GRUB? Edit GRUB entry and add 'single' for recovery. (3) Kernel: is it panicking? Add 'earlyprintk' to boot params. (4) Filesystem: is fsck failing? Boot from recovery media (live CD, rescue ISO) and check. (5) Systemd: is a service hanging? Add 'systemd.log_level=debug systemd.log_target=console' to boot params. At Enterprise, the first response is usually: attach the EBS volume to a healthy instance and repair from there — faster than debugging the failing boot process.'

Q: 'A team member accidentally deleted /etc/passwd on a production server. How do you recover?'

A: 'Don't reboot! The system is still running — as long as existing processes are alive, they keep their credentials. First, restore the file from backup: cp /var/backups/passwd /etc/passwd (if backups exist). If no backup, reconstruct from /etc/passwd- (a backup maintained by pwconv): cp /etc/passwd- /etc/passwd. If that doesn't exist, recreate users from getent passwd output captured on other servers, or reconstruct from /etc/shadow and /etc/group. At Enterprise, we have immutable infrastructure — /etc/passwd is managed by configuration management and restored automatically. If a file is deleted, the config management agent (Chef, Ansible) recreates it on the next run.'

Q: 'What would you include in a disaster recovery plan for a Linux-based service?'

A: 'A DR plan should cover: (1) Recovery objectives: RTO (Recovery Time Objective — how fast) and RPO (Recovery Point Objective — how much data loss). (2) Backup strategy: filesystem-level (duplicity/rsync), block-level (EBS snapshots), database-level (WAL archiving). (3) Recovery procedures: step-by-step runbooks for rebuilding a server, restoring from backup, promoting a replica. (4) Boot recovery: live USB images, PXE boot configuration, GRUB rescue procedures. (5) Testing: regular DR drills where the team actually goes through the recovery process. At Enterprise, DR is not a document — it's an automated process. We practice 'chaos engineering' where servers are intentionally terminated to verify auto-recovery. The manual DR procedures are for the 0.1% of cases where automation fails.'

• GRUB Rescue Shell — Official GRUB rescue documentation
  • Ubuntu Live CD Recovery — Community guide for chroot rescue procedures
  • Arch Linux Filesystem Repair — Comprehensive fsck and recovery reference

learningObjectives: Understand boot Process: From Power-On to Login Prompt

analogy.title: System Rescue as Emergency Surgery
analogy.description: Boot from live media (the ambulance). Mount the root filesystem (access the patient). chroot into it (operate in context). Fix fstab, reinstall grub, reset passwords, recover from backup. The goal: stabilize and discharge.

commonMistakes.solution: Always use `rm -i` or `ls` first to confirm what will be deleted

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Boot Process: From Power-On to Login Prompt
practiceLabs.task: Run `ls` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man ls` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: GRUB: Bootloader Rescue & Repair
practiceLabs.task: Run `cat` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man cat` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Chroot Rescue & Single-User Mode
practiceLabs.task: Run `sudo` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man sudo` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `ls` | Boot Process: From Power-On to Login Prompt |
| `cat` | Boot Process: From Power-On to Login Prompt |
| `sudo` | Boot Process: From Power-On to Login Prompt |
| `grub` | GRUB: Bootloader Rescue & Repair |
| `sudo` | GRUB: Bootloader Rescue & Repair |
| `grub-install` | GRUB: Bootloader Rescue & Repair |
| `mount` | Chroot Rescue & Single-User Mode |
| `lsblk` | Chroot Rescue & Single-User Mode |
| `sudo` | Chroot Rescue & Single-User Mode |
| `sudo` | Filesystem Recovery & Data Rescue |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man ls`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: system rescue operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.symptom: Permission issues accessing system rescue resources
troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is Boot Process: From Power-On to Login Prompt?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The Boot Process: From Power-On to Login Prompt is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `ls` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the system rescue directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a system rescue directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a system rescue scenario, your monitoring alerts show ls failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  System rescue is one of those skills that seems obsolete in the cloud era — until you need it
FAANG, we have automated recovery for 99% of scenarios: instance health checks, auto-scaling, self-healing Kubernetes, immutable infrastructure
productionTips: Production Tip:  Before you need it, create a rescue USB or ISO for your distribution and test it
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, system rescue & recovery is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
